title: Selecting Specific Columns
description: Don't grab the whole table. Get exactly what you need.

slides.title: Too Much Data! 💥
slides.content:
# The problem with the Star (*)

In the previous chapter, we learned that `SELECT *` fetches every single column in a table.

But what if your `users` table has 50 columns? Downloading all that data just to find a user's first name wastes a lot of time and internet bandwidth!

slides.title: Be Specific 🎯
slides.content:
## Comma-Separated Selections

Instead of `*`, you should explicitly list the exact columns you want, separated by commas.

For example, to get just the first and last names:
`SELECT first_name, last_name FROM users;`

quizData.question: If you only want to know the emails and phone numbers of your users, which query is the BEST choice?
quizData.explanation: Selecting only the exact columns you need (separated by commas) makes your database query drastically faster than pulling everything.

slides.title: The Order Matters ⏱️
slides.content:
## Re-arranging Output

The order in which you type the columns is the exact order the database will display them.

`SELECT age, first_name FROM users;` will display the age column first, then the name.

quizData.question: If I write `SELECT last_name, first_name FROM users;`, what order do the columns appear in the result?
quizData.explanation: The database strictly respects the order in which you typed the column names in your SELECT statement.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Retrieve the `title` and `release_year` of all movies.
